highest_level_of_school_completed: Some college, no degree (completed 2 years toward an Associate’s in Electrical Technology)
veteran: No, I have never served in the U.S. Armed Forces
indicate_any_special_accommodations_you_:
I have mild dyslexia that mainly affects my reading speed and my ability to follow along when a lot of information is presented only in dense text or very quickly on slides. I do well when I can see things broken down step-by-step, and when I can reference written instructions at my own pace.

To be successful in this apprenticeship, the accommodations that would help me most are:

1. Written instructions and checklists: When possible, I would like important procedures, safety protocols, and assignments to be provided in clear written form (bullet points/checklists work great for me). For example, if we are learning a new lockout/tagout procedure or wiring method, having a printed or digital step-by-step checklist I can keep with me on the job would help me double-check that I’m doing each step in the correct order.

2. Extra time for written tests and paperwork: I can complete written tests and forms independently, but I usually need a little more time than others to read and process the questions. An additional 25–30% time on written exams, safety quizzes, and any long written assessments would be very helpful and would reduce a lot of anxiety. I don’t need questions read out loud; I just need a bit more time to read carefully.

3. Clear, verbal explanations and opportunities to ask clarifying questions: I learn best when I can hear something explained and then immediately see it demonstrated. For example, if we are going over a wiring diagram or blueprint, walking through it verbally and visually before I’m expected to interpret it on my own is extremely helpful. I am not shy about asking questions, and I appreciate an environment where it’s okay to say, “Can you walk through that one more time?”

4. Digital copies of training materials: If possible, having access to digital versions (PDFs or slides) of manuals, diagrams, and training materials before or after class would help me review at home. I sometimes use text-to-speech tools on my computer to read longer passages out loud, which helps me catch details I might miss just reading silently.

5. Minimizing time-pressure when learning new tasks: When I’m first learning a hands-on task, I do better if I’m not rushed. For example, when first learning to bend conduit, terminate panels, or read multi-page schematics, having the chance to go at a slightly slower pace at the beginning lets me build accuracy and confidence. Once I’ve done something a few times, I can usually work at the normal pace without an issue.

To be clear, I am fully able to perform the physical aspects of the work (lifting, climbing ladders, working on my feet, etc.). My main need is just a bit of flexibility around how information is presented and how quickly I’m expected to process written materials. I’ve successfully completed technical coursework and hands-on labs in the past with similar accommodations and have consistently performed well when given these supports.
how_did_you_hear_about_this_apprenticesh:
I first heard about this apprenticeship program through a combination of word-of-mouth and an information session at my local community college.

Specifically, I was enrolled in the Electrical Technology program at Community College of Philadelphia from 2019 to 2021. During my second year, one of my instructors, Mr. Alvarez, mentioned that several of his former students had gone on to this exact apprenticeship and spoke highly of it. He pointed out that it was a good pathway for people who liked hands-on work, were interested in eventually becoming licensed journeyman electricians, and wanted to earn while they learned instead of taking on more student debt.

A few weeks later, representatives from the apprenticeship program visited our campus and hosted an evening information session. I attended that session in person. At the session, a program coordinator walked us through how the apprenticeship works (on-the-job training during the day and related technical instruction in the evenings), the typical schedule, the wage progression over the years, and the long-term career paths (journeyman, foreman, estimator, etc.). A current third-year apprentice also spoke about his experience, including the kinds of job sites he had been on (commercial buildings in Center City, a hospital renovation, and some light industrial work) and how the classroom training tied into what he was doing in the field.

After that session, I picked up a flyer that had the program’s website and contact information. Later that week, I went to the website, read through the requirements, and filled out an interest form. I also called the office phone number listed on the flyer to double-check that my previous coursework at the community college would be viewed positively and to ask about any upcoming application deadlines. The staff member I spoke with encouraged me to attend a more detailed orientation, which I did about a month later at their training center in Northeast Philadelphia.

On top of that, a friend of mine from high school, Jason, is currently in his second year of this same apprenticeship. He shared his experience with me over coffee—he talked about what the first year was like (a lot of basic wiring, learning to work safely around live panels, and getting comfortable working early hours), how supportive his journeyman mentors have been, and how the pay increases each year. Hearing from someone I know personally, combined with the information session and the college instructor’s recommendation, really convinced me that this was the right next step for me.

So overall, I learned about this apprenticeship through: 
- My community college instructor’s recommendation,
- A formal on-campus information session by program staff,
- The program’s official website and printed flyers,
- And personal conversations with a friend who is already in the program.
if_yes_which_pre_apprenticeship_program:
Yes. I completed the "Philadelphia Electrical Trades Pre-Apprenticeship Program" run in partnership between Community College of Philadelphia and the Philadelphia Workforce Development Board.

The program was a 16-week, part-time pre-apprenticeship that I attended from February 2023 through May 2023. It focused on introducing participants to the electrical trade and preparing us for entry into a registered apprenticeship. The curriculum included:

- Basic electrical theory (Ohm’s Law, series and parallel circuits, voltage, current, resistance)
- Hands-on labs wiring simple circuits, switches, and receptacles on training boards
- Safety training including OSHA-10 certification, lockout/tagout basics, ladder safety, PPE, and job-site hazard recognition
- Blueprint and schematic reading at a beginner level
- Construction math, including fractions, measurements, and conduit bending calculations
- Workplace readiness skills, such as resume writing, interview practice, and expectations for behavior and punctuality on job sites

As part of the program, we also visited two active job sites and the main training center for this apprenticeship. During those visits, we spoke with current apprentices and instructors, which is another reason I felt confident pursuing this path. At the end of the pre-apprenticeship, I received a certificate of completion, my OSHA-10 card, and letters of recommendation from the program coordinator and one of the lead instructors.

The pre-apprenticeship confirmed for me that I enjoy electrical work, can handle the physical demands, and am comfortable in a construction environment. It also gave me a foundation in safety and basic electrical concepts that I believe will help me hit the ground running in this apprenticeship.
